message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  COVID-19 survival data presents a special situation where not only the time-to-event period is short, but also the two events or outcome types, death and release from hospital, are mutually exclusive, leading to two cause-specific hazard ratios (csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ). The eventual mortality/release outcome can also be analyzed by logistic regression to obtain odds-ratio (OR). We have the following three empirical observations concerning csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  , csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and OR: (1) The magnitude of OR is an upper limit of the csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  : | log(OR) | ≥ | log(csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  )|. This relationship between OR and HR might be understood from the definition of the two quantities; (2) csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  point in opposite directions: log(csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  )· log(csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ) < 0; This relation is a direct consequence of the nature of the two events; and (3) there is a tendency for a reciprocal relation between csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  : csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ∼ 1/csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . Though an approximate reciprocal trend between the two hazard ratios is in indication that the same factor causing faster death also lead to slow recovery by a similar mechanism, and vice versa, a quantitative relation between csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and csHR
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  in this context is not obvious. These resutls may help future analyses of COVID-19 data, in particular if the deceased samples are lacking.
                </jats:p>
title: Approximate Reciprocal Relationship Between Two Cause-Specific Hazard Ratios in COVID-19 Data With Mutually Exclusive Events

reference.unstructured: S Cetin , A Ulgen , PO Balci , H Sivgin , M Cetin , S Sivgin , W Li (2021), Survival analyses of COVID-19 patients in a Turkish cohort: comparison between using time to death and time to release, SciMed J., to appear.

As their numbers grow, COVID-19 “long haulers” stump experts (news & analysis)
Odds ratio, hazard ratio and relative risk
Relative rates not relative risks: addressing a widespread misinterpretation of hazard ratios
